Gen 49:10), foreshadowing the Davidic line.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Iron chariots" are presented as the limit of human capability, not divine power. This verse is often misread as limiting God -- but the Hebrew places the failure on Judah's faith, not God's ability. verses.flow: Then the LORD raised up judges who delivered them from the hands of those who plundered them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The judge cycle is the structural backbone of the book: (1) Israel sins and serves other gods, (2) the LORD gives them to oppressors, (3) Israel cries out, (4) the LORD raises a deliverer (shophet), (5) the land has rest. But 2:19 reveals the spiral is downward -- each cycle ends worse than the last. The judges are saviors, but temporary and imperfect ones.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jotham's parable of the trees is the most sophisticated political allegory in the OT. The productive trees (olive, fig, vine) refuse kingship because they are already fruitful; only the worthless bramble (Abimelech) desires power. The parable is political theology: those who seek power are the least qualified to wield it. The bramble offers "shade" it cannot provide and threatens fire it will certainly bring.

verses.flow: then it shall be that whatever comes out of the doors of my house to meet me when I return in peace from the sons of Ammon, it shall be the LORD's, and I will offer it up as a burnt offering."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Jephthah's vow -- the scholarly debate: (1) The traditional reading: he sacrificed his daughter as a burnt offering (human sacrifice), reflecting how deeply Canaanite practices had corrupted Israel. (2) The alternative: "it shall be the LORD's" means permanent dedication to virginal service at the tabernacle, and "I will offer it up" is a separate vow of a burnt offering. Arguments for dedication: the text emphasizes her virginity (not her death), and she is "commemorated" (tanah) annually. Arguments for sacrifice: the Hebrew 'olah unambiguously means "burnt offering," and Jephthah comes from a pagan-influenced region. Either reading serves the book's theme: Israel is descending into moral chaos.

The Angel refuses to give His name because it is beyond human comprehension -- He is the pre-incarnate Word whose full revelation awaits the Incarnation. His ascension in the flame (13:20) confirms His divine nature.

verses.flow: And the Spirit of the LORD rushed upon him, so that he tore it apart as one tears apart a young goat, though he had nothing in his hand; but he did not tell his father or mother what he had done.
verses.note: HEBREW: tsalach = "rush upon" -- the Spirit of the LORD "rushed upon" Samson. This is different from the Spirit "coming upon" other judges. Tsalach implies sudden, overwhelming, almost violent empowerment. It occurs three times with Samson (14:6, 14:19, 15:14) and is the same verb used of Saul (1 Sam 10:6). Samson's strength is always episodic, not permanent.

verses.flow: And she said, "The Philistines are upon you, Samson!" And he awoke from his sleep and said, "I will go out as at other times and shake myself free." But he did not know that the LORD had departed from him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "But he did not know that the LORD had departed from him" -- the most chilling verse in Judges. Samson's tragedy is not the loss of hair but the loss of divine presence without awareness. This is the spiritual death that precedes physical captivity. The pattern applies to all of Israel: they lose God's presence gradually, without noticing.

verses.flow: Then Samson called to the LORD and said, "O Lord GOD, please remember me and please strengthen me just this once, O God, that I may at once be avenged of the Philistines for my two eyes."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Samson's final prayer is his most spiritually mature moment -- he calls on the LORD by covenant name (Adonai YHWH). His death, bringing down the temple of Dagon on himself and the Philistines, kills more enemies than his entire life. CHRIST TYPOLOGY: Samson's death -- arms outstretched between two pillars, dying to destroy God's enemies -- is an imperfect but striking type of Christ's death on the cross. Both achieve their greatest victory in death.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "In those days there was no king in Israel; everyone did what was right in his own eyes." This is the thesis statement of chapters 17-21 and the verdict on the entire book. verses.flow: When he entered his house, he took a knife and laid hold of his concubine and cut her in twelve pieces, limb by limb, and sent her throughout the territory of Israel.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The dismemberment of the concubine into twelve pieces (one for each tribe) is a grim parody of covenant-making. In ANE treaty ceremonies, animals were cut in pieces as a self-curse (cf. Gen 15:10-17). The Levite turns a murdered woman into a covenant summons. The horror is the point: Israel must see what it has become.

verses.flow: Now the sons of Israel arose, went up to Bethel, and inquired of God, and said, "Who shall go up first for us to battle against the sons of Benjamin?" Then the LORD said, "Judah shall go up first."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Israel inquires of the LORD before battle, and God says "Judah first" -- echoing 1:1-2. But this time it is civil war, not conquest. God allows Israel to suffer devastating defeats (20:21, 25) before granting victory. The losses purge Israel of self-righteousness -- they too had become compromised. The final victory comes only after fasting, weeping, and burnt offerings (20:26).

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The book's final verse and ultimate verdict: "In those days there was no king in Israel; everyone did what was right in his own eyes." This is not merely a statement of political anarchy but of spiritual autonomy. The book of Judges is the theological argument for kingship -- specifically for a godly king who will enforce covenant faithfulness. CHRIST TYPOLOGY: The book's cry for a king finds its ultimate answer not in Saul or David but in Christ, the eternal King who will finally end the cycle of sin, oppression, and partial deliverance.
